title: Cat Refusing to Put Weight on Left Hind Leg: What to Do
meta_desc: Learn why your cat is limping on its left hind leg. Discover urgency levels, common causes, and how photos help your vet provide a faster diagnosis.
content_html:
TL;DR: A cat refusing to put weight on its leg is in significant pain and requires a veterinary exam within 24 hours, or immediate emergency care if the limb is cold, dragging, or if the cat is vocalizing.

What does it mean if my cat is not putting weight on its leg?

If your cat is completely avoiding using their left hind leg, it indicates a high level of discomfort or a structural inability to support their weight. This condition is often caused by:

  • Physical trauma
  • A deep puncture wound leading to an abscess
  • A sudden orthopedic injury such as a ligament tear or fracture

When does my cat need to see a vet for a leg injury?

A cat refusing to put weight on a limb is in pain and requires a professional examination. Follow these urgency guidelines:

  • Schedule a visit with your veterinarian within 24 hours for standard lameness.
  • Monitor for severe signs like the limb dragging or feeling cold to the touch.
  • Visit an emergency clinic immediately if your cat is crying out in pain.

How does sending a photo help my vet triage my cat's injury?

Providing your vet with a photo or a short video is incredibly helpful for remote triage. Visual evidence helps the medical team in the following ways:

  • Allows the team to look for swelling or abnormal angles.
  • Helps identify hidden wounds, like bite marks, that might be obscured by fur.
  • Enables the team to prioritize your appointment based on the visible severity of the injury.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

When a cat refuses to put weight on its left hind leg, a thorough lameness examination is warranted, assessing the limb from distal to proximal. Palpate bones, joints, and soft tissues, noting any swelling, pain, instability, crepitation, reduced range of motion, or muscle atrophy. Observe the cat's stance and gait for abnormalities such as a shortened stride on the affected side or a dropped head during weight bearing. Serial examinations before and after exercise may be necessary for subtle lameness. Diagnostic imaging, including radiography, ultrasonography, CT, or MRI, may be required to determine the underlying cause. Sedation may be necessary for fractious animals to facilitate palpation, radiography, and arthrocentesis.

Chapter: Cardiology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 127)

faqs.question: Can I wait a few days to see if it gets better?
faqs.answer: It is not recommended to wait. Because cats are masters at hiding pain, a cat that is completely non-weight-bearing is experiencing significant distress and needs professional relief.

faqs.question: What are common causes of hind leg limping?
faqs.answer: Common causes include soft tissue sprains, fractures, luxating patellas, or infected bite wounds from another animal.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human pain medication?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your cat human medications like ibuprofen or acetaminophen, as these are highly toxic to felines and can be fatal.
